skill.name: config-auditing
skill.description: Audit Neovim configurations for structure, performance, security, compatibility, and deprecated API usage using documented checklists and examples.
skill.summary: Review Neovim configs for common issues, deprecated APIs, and maintainability improvements.
skill.author: BitYoungjae

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many high-risk patterns, but review shows they are mostly Markdown audit examples, grep patterns, and Neovim configuration snippets. No malicious intent, prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or hidden executable payload was found. The skill remains medium risk because following the documented checks can run shell commands and headless Neovim against user configuration files.

medium_findings.title: Headless Neovim Commands Can Execute Local Configuration
medium_findings.description: The documentation suggests running Neovim headless commands and loading init.lua for validation. This is legitimate for a Neovim audit, but it can execute user configuration code and plugin hooks on the local machine.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly documented audit steps, not hidden payloads. Confidence is high that execution can occur, but the surrounding context supports legitimate diagnostic use.

medium_findings.title: Audit Commands Read User Configuration Files
medium_findings.description: Several examples recursively inspect ~/.config/nvim and related Neovim directories. This can expose local paths, plugin names, and possible secret matches in assistant output if results are shared broadly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem access is explicit and tied to configuration auditing. It is not malicious, but it can reveal sensitive local configuration details during normal use.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Are Mostly Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The scanner reported many shell execution and weak-crypto indicators. Manual review found Markdown code blocks, grep patterns, tables, and examples rather than auto-running scripts or concealed execution paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are inside Markdown documentation and examples. No code in the skill automatically invokes these commands.

low_findings.title: Secret Pattern Matches Are Audit Guidance, Not Embedded Secrets
low_findings.description: The secret-related matches are grep patterns used to find credentials in a user configuration, plus a recommendation to use environment variables. No actual API key, token, or password was found in the skill text.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines describe how to detect exposed credentials and how to fix them. They do not contain real secrets or exfiltration behavior.

low_findings.title: Obfuscation Match Is Regex Escaping In A Keymap Audit
low_findings.description: The hex-encoded character alerts point to escaped quote matching in a sed expression for duplicate keymap detection. This appears to be normal shell pattern syntax, not concealment.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The escaped character appears in a documented sed command that extracts keymap text. The surrounding section is a benign duplicate-keymap audit.

dangerous_patterns.title: Headless Editor Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples use nvim --headless to inspect version, startup state, modeline settings, and exrc settings. These commands should only be run on configurations the user intends to audit.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is real and operationally meaningful. Context shows diagnostic intent rather than malicious automation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Recursive Local Configuration Search
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples use grep and find against ~/.config/nvim and derived config directories. Results can include sensitive local paths or matched secret-like values.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The local filesystem reads are visible in documented commands. The commands are scoped to Neovim configuration auditing and are not exfiltrating data.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Script Audit Helper
dangerous_patterns.description: deprecated-apis.md includes a Bash audit helper for deprecated Neovim APIs. It is documentation, but users should review commands before running them.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The script is clearly presented as an optional quick audit helper. It performs local grep commands and does not include network transfer or destructive actions.

content.value_statement: Neovim configs often collect deprecated APIs, slow startup paths, and unsafe shell patterns over time. This skill provides structured checks, best practices, and version-aware guidance for cleaner configs.
actual_capabilities: Guides structured Neovim configuration reviews across organization, performance, security, compatibility, and redundancy.
Provides command examples for checking Neovim version, config location, startup time, and deprecated API usage.
Explains lazy.nvim configuration patterns, loading triggers, dependencies, and migration notes from packer.nvim.
Lists deprecated Neovim APIs by version with replacement guidance for Lua configuration updates.
Offers a grading model for summarizing audit severity and overall configuration health.
limitations: It is a documentation skill, not an automated scanner with its own parser.
Command examples may execute local Neovim configuration when the user chooses to run them.
Deprecated API guidance may need updates as future Neovim releases change behavior.
It focuses on Neovim Lua configuration and may not cover every plugin-specific rule.

use_cases.title: Review A Personal Neovim Setup
use_cases.description: Find slow startup paths, duplicate settings, unsafe shell usage, and deprecated APIs before making larger config changes.

use_cases.title: Modernize A Shared Editor Config
use_cases.description: Compare a team Neovim setup against lazy.nvim and Lua best practices while preserving stable behavior.

use_cases.title: Prepare For Neovim Version Upgrades
use_cases.description: Check version-specific deprecations and removed APIs before upgrading developer machines or base images.

prompt_templates.title: Run A Basic Config Audit
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a local Neovim setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit my Neovim configuration for structure, startup performance, security risks, deprecated APIs, and duplicate settings. Ask before running shell commands.

prompt_templates.title: Check Deprecated APIs
prompt_templates.scenario: Compatibility review for a known Neovim version
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Neovim Lua files for APIs deprecated or removed in Neovim [version]. Provide replacements and prioritize breaking issues first.

prompt_templates.title: Improve lazy.nvim Configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect my lazy.nvim plugin specs for eager loading, dependency issues, missing triggers, and config patterns that should use opts.

prompt_templates.title: Produce A Release-Ready Audit Report
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced audit for a team configuration repository
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a graded Neovim configuration audit report with critical issues, warnings, suggestions, affected files, migration steps, and validation commands.

output_examples.input: Audit a Neovim config before upgrading to version 0.10.
output: A prioritized report of removed or deprecated APIs that affect version 0.10.
Replacement guidance for each affected API, with notes about compatibility risk.
A short validation plan for checking startup behavior after changes.
A list of plugins that appear to load too early or lack clear triggers.
Recommendations for event, command, filetype, or key-based loading.
A summary of changes likely to reduce startup work.

output_examples.input: Assess a shared Neovim configuration repository.
output: A grade with critical issues, warnings, and suggestions.
Security notes for shell commands, modelines, exrc, and exposed credentials.
Actionable cleanup steps for structure, compatibility, and duplicate settings.

best_practices: Review command examples before running them, especially commands that start Neovim or read home directory files.
Provide the target Neovim version so deprecated API checks can be applied accurately.
Audit in stages: structure first, then performance, security, compatibility, and redundancy.
anti_patterns: Running every suggested shell command without checking whether it matches the local environment.
Treating grep matches as confirmed defects without reading the surrounding configuration context.
Applying version-specific replacements without confirming the Neovim version used by the team.

faq.question: Does this skill automatically change my Neovim files?
faq.answer: No. It provides audit guidance and recommendations. File changes require separate user approval and editing steps.

faq.question: Can it detect deprecated Neovim APIs?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes version-specific deprecated API patterns and replacement guidance for common Neovim releases.

faq.question: Why is the security risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill is documentation, but its examples can run shell commands and headless Neovim against local configuration files.

faq.question: Does it support lazy.nvim?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes lazy.nvim patterns for opts, config functions, loading triggers, dependencies, and plugin organization.

faq.question: Can it audit plugin-specific behavior?
faq.answer: It can identify common configuration patterns, but plugin-specific rules may require additional documentation or source review.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks the skill as compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
